bids.reasoning: This is the opening bid at t=0, so I should start with my maximum utility offer. All issues have the same utility structure, so valueF gives 1.0 utility on every issue. My top bid gives utility of 0.1*1.0 + 0.2*1.0 + 0.3*1.0 + 0.4*1.0 = 1.0. Starting at the best possible position is standard early-game strategy with no time pressure yet.

The opponent opened with their best offer (all valueA), which gives me minimum utility (0.2 across all issues = 0.2 total). We're very early in the negotiation (t=0.02), so there's no need to concede much. I'll stay close to my top bid but make a small concession on issueA (my least important issue, weight 0.1) to signal willingness to negotiate, while holding firm on issueD (most important, weight 0.4) and issueC (weight 0.3).

The opponent is firmly holding at their maximum utility bid (all valueA) and hasn't conceded at all. It's very early (t=0.04), so I should stay near my top utility while making small concessions. I'll concede slightly on issueA (my least important issue at 0.1 weight) by dropping to valueD, and keep my high-value choices on issueD (most important) and issueC. This keeps my utility high while signaling willingness to negotiate.

The opponent has been offering all-A values but just shifted issueC and issueD to valueE, signaling they care more about issueA and issueB (keeping those at valueA) while being willing to give ground on issueC and issueD. Since issueD (weight 0.4) and issueC (weight 0.3) are my most important issues, I want to hold firm on those. The opponent's latest offer gives me utility: 0.1*0.2 + 0.2*0.2 + 0.3*0.84 + 0.4*0.84 = 0.02+0.04+0.252+0.336 = 0.648, which is decent but I can do better. It's still very early (t=0.06), so I'll make a small concession on issueA (low importance to me) while holding firm on issueD and issueC at valueF, and slightly concede issueB.

The opponent's latest offer gives me utility: issueA=valueA(0.1×0.20=0.02) + issueB=valueC(0.2×0.52=0.104) + issueC=valueF(0.3×1.00=0.30) + issueD=valueE(0.4×0.84=0.336) = ~0.76. They seem to be conceding on issueC and issueB while holding firm on issueA and issueD. It's still early (t=0.08), so I should stay near my top utility but make a small concession. I'll hold firm on issueD (my highest weight) at valueF, keep issueC at valueF, concede slightly on issueB to valueE, and give issueA=valueC since it's low weight.

The opponent's latest offer gives me: issueA=valueB (0.1×0.35=0.035), issueB=valueA (0.2×0.20=0.04), issueC=valueE (0.3×0.84=0.252), issueD=valueF (0.4×1.00=0.40) = ~0.727 utility. The opponent seems to be conceding on issueC and issueD (their most recent offers show valueE and valueF for these), which aligns well with my high-weight issues. I should hold firm on issueD (my highest weight) and issueC, while making small concessions on issueA and issueB where my weight is lower. At t=0.10 we're still early, so I'll maintain a strong position but vary slightly from my last bid.

The opponent's latest offer (issueA: valueD, issueB: valueA, issueC: valueF, issueD: valueF) gives me utility: 0.1*0.68 + 0.2*0.20 + 0.3*1.00 + 0.4*1.00 = 0.068 + 0.04 + 0.30 + 0.40 = 0.808. That's actually quite good for me! The opponent seems to be conceding on issueC and issueD (both now at valueF), which are my most important issues. I should counter with something that keeps my high-value issues (issueC, issueD at valueF) while making a small concession on issueB to signal willingness to deal, but it's still very early (t=0.12) so I don't need to rush. I'll offer issueA: valueC, issueB: valueD, issueC: valueF, issueD: valueF for utility: 0.1*0.52 + 0.2*0.68 + 0.3*1.00 + 0.4*1.00 = 0.052 + 0.136 + 0.30 + 0.40 = 0.888.

The opponent's latest offer (issueA: valueB, issueB: valueD, issueC: valueF, issueD: valueE) gives me utility: 0.1*0.35 + 0.2*0.68 + 0.3*1.00 + 0.4*0.84 = 0.035 + 0.136 + 0.300 + 0.336 = 0.807. That's actually quite good! The opponent seems to be converging toward higher values on issueC and issueD (my most important issues). I should counter with something close but slightly better for me - keeping issueC: valueF and issueD: valueF (my top priorities), while conceding a bit on issueA and issueB since those matter less to me. The opponent seems to like valueD on issueB and valueF on issueC, so I'll keep those and push issueD to valueF while giving a bit on issueA.

The opponent's latest offer (t=0.16) is {issueA: valueA, issueB: valueE, issueC: valueF, issueD: valueF}, which gives me utility: 0.1*0.20 + 0.2*0.84 + 0.3*1.00 + 0.4*1.00 = 0.02 + 0.168 + 0.30 + 0.40 = 0.888. That's actually quite good! The opponent seems to be converging toward valueF on issueC and issueD (my highest-weight issues), while varying issueA and issueB. I should make a competitive counter that holds firm on issueC and issueD (valueF) while making a small concession on issueB to signal willingness to deal, keeping my utility high since we're still early in the negotiation.

The opponent's latest offer (issueA: valueB, issueB: valueD, issueC: valueF, issueD: valueF) gives me utility: 0.1*0.35 + 0.2*0.68 + 0.3*1.0 + 0.4*1.0 = 0.035 + 0.136 + 0.3 + 0.4 = 0.871. That's actually quite good for me! The opponent seems to be converging toward offers that are favorable to me on issueC and issueD (the most important issues). My last offer was issueA: valueB, issueB: valueD, issueC: valueF, issueD: valueF with utility 0.871 as well. The opponent's latest offer matches my last bid almost exactly - I should accept or make a very similar counter. Since we're only at t=0.18, I'll hold firm on issueC and issueD (valueF) and try to improve issueB slightly while keeping issueA at valueB, essentially mirroring back a strong offer.
